sections.heading: Scope of application
sections.title: Financial institutions covered by cyber risk management guidelines
sections.summary: This section explicitly states that the guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank of Mauritius. It specifies that the effective date is 29 May 2023. The section defines key terms such as 'financial institution' to include banks, non-bank deposit-taking institutions, payment service providers, and operators of payment systems, clearing, and settlement systems. It also provides detailed definitions for terms like 'cyber and technology event', 'cyber and technology incident', and 'data breach' to ensure clarity in implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the scope of the guideline, defining which financial institutions are subject to cyber risk management requirements and specifying critical terms like 'data breach' that directly impact data transfer assessments. It sets the foundation for understanding what constitutes a cyber incident and breach in the context of data transfers.

requirements.text: This guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank.
This guideline shall come into effect on 29 May 2023.

sections.source_quote: This guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank. Effective date: This guideline shall come into effect on 29 May 2023.

sections.heading: Scope of application (cont.)
sections.title: Additional definitions for cyber risk management terms
sections.summary: This section provides further definitions for terms such as 'restoration', 'scenario-based testing', 'security zone', and 'third-party service provider' to clarify the scope and application of the cyber risk management framework. It ensures that all stakeholders have a consistent understanding of critical concepts like vulnerability assessment and threat intelligence, which are essential for implementing robust cyber resilience measures.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify terms that directly impact data transfer assessments, particularly 'data breach' and 'cyber and technology incident', which are critical for evaluating the risks associated with cross-border data transfers and third-party service providers.

requirements.text: Restoration is the process of copying backup data from secondary storage and restoring it to its primary location or a new location.
Scenario-based testing refers to a test that assesses a financial institution's response, resumption and recovery plans in order to strengthen cyber resilience.
Security zone refers to a logical segmented section of a network that contains computer or computer systems which have been grouped in accordance with specific security requirements set.
Third-party service provider refers to an entity that is undertaking an activity on behalf of the financial institution or providing a service to the financial institution.

sections.source_quote: Restoration is the process of copying backup data from secondary storage and restoring it to its primary location or a new location.

sections.heading: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and
sections.title: Board must promote cyber risk awareness culture
sections.summary: This section mandates that the board and senior management must actively foster a strong culture of awareness regarding cyber and technology risk management within the financial institution. It emphasizes the importance of embedding risk awareness at all levels to ensure effective risk management practices and proactive identification of potential threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This requirement ensures that the institution has a culture of awareness for cyber risks, which is critical for identifying and mitigating risks associated with data transfers and third-party services.

requirements.text: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and technology risk management throughout the institution.

sections.source_quote: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and technology risk management throughout the institution.

sections.heading: Both the board and senior management must collectively possess adequate expertise and
sections.title: Permanent data irrecoverability assurance
sections.summary: Financial institutions must obtain assurance from third-party service providers through independent reports or certificates that all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible within a timely period after contract termination. This requirement applies to all third-party service providers handling financial institution data.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party service providers can securely delete data upon contract termination to prevent residual data exposure in cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Obtain assurance from third-party service provider through independent reports/certificates
Ensure all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible after contract termination

sections.source_quote: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant independent reports/certificates that all data of the financial institution (including any backup) is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible in a timely manner after termination of the contract;

sections.heading: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant
sections.title: Board must include cyber-experienced independent directors
sections.summary: This section specifies that the board must include at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology matters. For Domestic Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs), the board must have at least two such directors. Branches of foreign banks with local advisory boards must ensure that at least one board member has relevant cyber expertise.
sections.dtia_summary: This requirement ensures that the board has specialized knowledge to oversee cyber risks, which is critical for evaluating the security of data transfers and third-party services.

requirements.text: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

The board of local banks categorised as Domestic Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs) shall comprise at least two independent/non-executive directors with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

Branches of foreign banks having a local advisory board/committee should ensure that the aforesaid board/committee include at least one member with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.source_quote: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.heading: The board and senior management must ensure that a comprehensive technology strategy
sections.title: Business continuity alternative solutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify alternative solutions to maintain business continuity throughout and after the transition phase when outsourcing services. This includes assessing risks, vulnerabilities, criticality of assets, impact of process changes, and ensuring data segregation and encryption for customer information.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must evaluate alternative solutions for business continuity to ensure uninterrupted data processing and transfer capabilities during third-party transitions.

requirements.text: Identify alternative solutions for business continuity throughout and after transition phase
Assess cyber and technology resilience capabilities of third-party service providers
Identify associated risks including cyber/IT related risks and concentration risks
Evaluate vulnerabilities, benefits, and sustainability of services
Evaluate impact of changes to processes and procedures
Evaluate adequacy of internal cyber/technology risk management framework
Ensure data, including customer information, is logically segregated and encrypted
Identify roles and accountabilities for financial institution and third-party service provider
Assess adequacy of control framework
Evaluate impact of possible risk events including third-party failure
Assess adequacy of contingency and exit plans including data portability
Evaluate risk of foreign authorities accessing data
Ensure compliance with relevant legal and regulatory requirements
Ensure third-party service provider complies with guideline requirements
Assess third-party service provider's track record

sections.source_quote: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity throughout and after the transition phase;

sections.heading: The board is responsible for:
sections.title: Board responsibilities for cyber and technology risk oversight
sections.summary: The board must annually review and approve technology strategy, cyber risk management frameworks, and related policies. It must approve a cyber risk appetite statement defining acceptable risk levels for business goals. The board must receive regular reports on cyber incidents, threat landscapes, audit findings, and risk management effectiveness. It must ensure a CISO is appointed with board approval and oversee risk management through sub-committees and a culture of resilience.
sections.dtia_summary: Board oversight of cyber risk frameworks and incident reporting is critical for DTIAs to ensure robust cross-border data handling practices and timely threat response.

requirements.text: Review and approve at least annually the technology strategy and cyber risk management framework
Approve a cyber risk appetite statement defining acceptable risk levels for business goals
Receive regular reports on material cyber incidents and threat landscape evolution
Ensure timely reports on audit findings and risk management framework effectiveness
Ensure appointment of a Chief Information Security Officer with board approval
Ensure adequate oversight of cyber risks by control functions and external auditors
Promote a culture of cyber and technology resilience

sections.source_quote: The board is responsible for: reviewing and approving at least annually or more frequently depending on the threat landscape, the technology strategy, the relevant cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework and related policies;

sections.heading: The board of local banks categorised as a D-SIB shall set up a distinct cyber and technology
sections.title: D-SIB banks must establish cyber risk sub-committee
sections.summary: D-SIB (Systemically Important Banks) must create a dedicated cyber and technology risk sub-committee with at least two directors. This sub-committee oversees technology strategy, risk management frameworks, and critical projects. It must receive regular reports and report quarterly to the board on material developments.
sections.dtia_summary: D-SIB banks require specific sub-committee oversight for cyber risks, impacting DTIAs by mandating structured cross-border data handling protocols.

requirements.text: Establish a distinct cyber and technology risk sub-committee with at least two directors
Ensure sub-committee oversees technology strategy and cyber risk management frameworks
Receive regular reports on cyber/technology matters from stakeholders
Report quarterly to the board on material developments

sections.source_quote: The board of local banks categorised as a D-SIB shall set up a distinct cyber and technology risk sub-committee comprising at least two directors with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.heading: The board of financial institutions other than local banks categorised as a D-SIB may, at its
sections.title: Non-D-SIB banks may delegate cyber oversight
sections.summary: Financial institutions not categorized as D-SIBs may delegate cyber risk responsibilities to the Risk Management Committee or a sub-committee. These committees must submit quarterly reports to the board and ensure timely communication of material developments.
sections.dtia_summary: Non-systemically important banks can delegate cyber oversight, but must maintain quarterly reporting to the board for DTIA compliance.

requirements.text: May delegate cyber risk responsibilities to Risk Management Committee or sub-committee
Submit quarterly reports to the board
Ensure timely communication of material developments to the board

sections.source_quote: The board of financial institutions other than local banks categorised as a D-SIB may, at its discretion, delegate the above roles and responsibilities to the Risk Management Committee or to an appropriate sub-committee of the board.

sections.heading: Senior management is responsible for:
sections.title: Senior management responsibilities for cyber risk
sections.summary: Senior management must implement technology strategies and cyber risk frameworks within board-defined risk appetite. They must define staff roles, develop risk metrics, review frameworks annually, monitor threats, assess risk management effectiveness, establish training programs, and escalate material concerns to the board.
sections.dtia_summary: Senior management must ensure cyber risk frameworks align with cross-border data handling requirements through regular reviews and threat monitoring.

requirements.text: Implement technology strategy and cyber risk management framework
Ensure cyber risk levels remain within board-defined appetite and tolerance
Define roles and responsibilities for cyber risk management staff
Develop key performance metrics for cyber risk monitoring
Review technology strategy and risk frameworks at least annually
Monitor cyber threats and developments for timely decision-making
Assess risk management framework effectiveness annually or after major changes
Establish cyber risk awareness and training programs for staff and board
Validate effectiveness of training and awareness programs
Promptly escalate material concerns to the board and sub-committees
Ensure staff have adequate skills and experience for cyber risk management
Collect and analyze cyber incident data and threat intelligence trends

sections.source_quote: Senior management is responsible for: the effective implementation of the technology strategy and of the cyber and technology risk management framework and strategy;

sections.heading: The CISO shall:
sections.title: CISO appointment and independence requirements
sections.summary: The CISO must be a senior officer, part of the second line of defense, independent of IT operations, possess relevant expertise, have sufficient resources, and maintain direct access to the board and designated sub-committees.
sections.dtia_summary: CISO independence and expertise are critical for DTIAs to ensure effective cross-border data security and incident response.

requirements.text: Be a senior officer
Be in the second line of defense
Be independent of IT operations
Have adequate skills, expertise and experience in cyber/technology risk management
Have sufficient resources including competent personnel
Maintain direct access to the board and designated sub-committees

sections.source_quote: The CISO shall: be a senior officer; be in the second line of defence; be independent of IT operations;

sections.heading: The CISO shall be responsible for:
sections.title: CISO responsibilities for cyber risk management
sections.summary: The CISO must develop and implement cyber risk frameworks, provide quarterly reports to the board, keep the Chief Risk Officer informed, support board and management, monitor policy compliance, ensure third-party oversight, design contingency plans, implement resilience measures, handle cyber queries, investigate incidents, monitor threat landscapes, and develop training programs.
sections.dtia_summary: CISO responsibilities directly impact DTIAs through mandatory quarterly reporting and third-party oversight requirements for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Develop and implement cyber and technology risk management framework
Provide quarterly reports on cyber risk position to board or sub-committees
Keep Chief Risk Officer informed of cyber/technology risk issues
Support board and management on cyber risk matters
Monitor compliance with cyber risk policies and procedures
Ensure adequate oversight of third-party service providers
Monitor third-party services for cyber risk management
Design and update contingency plans for cyber issues
Implement cyber resilience measures
Ensure process for reporting and addressing cyber queries
Investigate cyber incidents
Monitor evolution of threat landscape and emerging risks
Develop cyber and technology security awareness and training programs

sections.source_quote: The CISO shall be responsible for: the development and implementation of the cyber and technology risk management framework;

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust the roles and responsibilities of the
sections.title: CISO roles for foreign bank branches: delegation to senior officers
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may delegate CISO roles to senior officers in the second line of defense, independent of IT operations. The designated officer must report to the group or regional CISO and leverage group resources. The board must ensure these arrangements meet guideline requirements and provide adequate oversight through the officer, senior management, and board.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires delegation of CISO responsibilities to non-IT senior officers with board oversight, impacting how financial institutions structure cross-border risk management governance for foreign entities.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust CISO roles to an appropriate senior officer in the second line of defense
The designated senior officer must report to the group or regional CISO
The designated senior officer may rely on resources of the group/regional office
The board must ensure arrangements address all guideline requirements
The board must ensure adequate oversight by the designated officer, senior management, and board

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust the roles and responsibilities of the CISO to an appropriate senior officer in the second line of defense and independent of IT operations. The designated senior officer shall report to the group or regional CISO and may rely on the resources of the group/regional office. The board shall ensure that the arrangements in place address all the requirements of the guideline and that there is adequate oversight by the designated officer, the senior management and the board.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy which takes into
sections.title: Board-approved technology strategy for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved technology strategy that aligns with organizational objectives, incorporates input from all departments, and includes vision, technology roadmaps, project timelines, and budgetary details for short and long-term technology goals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a comprehensive technology strategy with clear timelines and budgets, which is critical for assessing the technical and financial risks associated with cross-border data transfers in financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy
The technology strategy must take into consideration the objectives of the entire organization
The technology strategy must incorporate input from all departments
The strategy must include vision for short-term (up to three years) and long-term technology goals
The strategy must detail technology roadmaps for short-term and long-term objectives
The strategy must specify timelines for critical projects
The strategy must include associated costs and budgets

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy which takes into consideration the objectives of the entire organization and input from all departments and includes: their vision, including where they are currently and where they aspire to reach over the short term (up to three years) and long term with respect to technology; their technology road map detailing the goals and objectives and the steps to be taken to achieve their vision over short term and long-term horizon; timelines for critical projects; and the associated costs and budgets.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management
sections.title: Board-approved cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework, and related policies to ensure systematic identification, mitigation, and oversight of cyber and technology risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires a formalized risk management framework for cyber and technology risks, which is essential for evaluating the security and compliance posture of financial institutions during data transfers.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework and related policies

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework and related policies.

sections.heading: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies
sections.title: Option for standalone or integrated cyber risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions may choose to have a standalone cyber and technology risk management framework and policies or integrate them into their enterprise-wide risk management framework, providing flexibility in risk governance structures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows financial institutions to either maintain a dedicated cyber risk framework or incorporate it into broader enterprise risk management, influencing how data transfer risks are assessed and mitigated.

requirements.text: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies for cyber and technology risk management
Financial institutions may include cyber and technology risk management in their enterprise-wide risk management framework

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies for cyber and technology risk management framework or include same in their Enterprise-Wide Risk Management Framework.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards
sections.title: Adoption of industry best practices for risk frameworks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must align their cyber and technology risk management frameworks with industry best practices and relevant standards, considering operational complexity, technology infrastructure, and inherent risks when implementing solutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes the importance of industry standards in risk management, which directly impacts the security and compliance of data transfers across borders for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards
Financial institutions must implement the most effective solutions considering operational complexity, technology, and inherent risks

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards when designing their frameworks and implement the most effective solutions taking into consideration, inter alia, the complexity of their operations and services, the underlying technology and inherent risks.

sections.heading: The cyber and technology risk management framework of a financial institution should:
sections.title: Cyber risk management framework requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure their cyber and technology risk management frameworks align with business strategy, risk appetite, documented processes, internal controls, and evolving threat landscapes while specifying roles, responsibilities, and third-party interconnections.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific requirements for cyber risk frameworks, including alignment with business strategy and threat landscapes, which are critical for assessing the security of cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: The framework must take into consideration the business strategy and technology strategy as approved by the board and the requirements of the guideline
The framework must be consistent with the cyber and technology risk appetite and tolerance statement approved by the board
The framework must document the processes behind the determination of cyber and technology risk management objectives and risk tolerance
The framework must document policies, procedures and controls taking into consideration the requirements of this guideline
The framework must be consistent with the overall risk management framework of the financial institution

The framework must take into consideration the current and evolving threat landscape, complexity of operations, services, technology and risks posed by vendors, market participants and service providers
The framework must clearly specify roles and responsibilities including accountability for decision-making in cyber and technology risk management
The framework must consider interconnections with third parties from a cyber and technology risk perspective
The framework must be communicated to relevant staff of the financial institution

sections.source_quote: The cyber and technology risk management framework of a financial institution should: take into consideration the business strategy and technology strategy as approved by the board and the requirements of the guideline; be consistent with the cyber and technology risk appetite and tolerance statement approved by the board; document the processes behind the determination of the cyber and technology risk management objectives and risk tolerance; document the policies, procedures and controls taking into consideration the requirements of this guideline; be consistent with the overall risk management framework of the financial institution; take into consideration the current and evolving threat landscape, the complexity of the operations, services and technology and risks posed by vendors, other market participants and service providers; clearly specify the roles and the responsibilities including accountability for decision-making in respect of identifying, mitigating and managing cyber and technology risks; consider the interconnections of the institution with third parties from a cyber and technology risk point of view; and be communicated to relevant staff of the financial institution.

sections.heading: The cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, including relevant
sections.title: Annual review of cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions must review and update their cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework annually or after significant changes in threats, information systems, or new services/technologies. This review must reflect evolving threats, threat intelligence, and new service providers to ensure the framework remains current and effective. The framework includes relevant policies and procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular updates to the risk management framework to address evolving threats, ensuring data transfers and processing activities remain aligned with current cybersecurity risks.

requirements.text: Review and update cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework annually
Review and update framework upon major changes in threat landscape or information system
Ensure framework reflects current and evolving threat landscape, threat intelligence, and new services/technologies

sections.source_quote: The cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, including relevant policies and procedures, shall be reviewed and updated annually, or upon major changes in the threat landscape or in the information system, to reflect the current and evolving threat landscape, threat intelligence and the introduction of new services, technologies and service providers.

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt the cyber and technology strategy and
sections.title: Foreign bank branches may adopt parent bank's cyber strategy
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may adopt their parent bank's cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, provided it aligns with the Guideline's requirements and their own technology and business strategies. This allows for consistency while ensuring compliance with local regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows foreign banks to leverage parent bank frameworks but requires alignment with local regulatory requirements for data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt the cyber and technology strategy and risk management framework, including relevant policies of their parent bank
Adopted framework must be in line with the requirements of this Guideline
Adopted framework must align with their technology and business strategies

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt the cyber and technology strategy and risk management framework, including relevant policies of their parent bank provided that they are in line with the requirements of this Guideline and their technology and business strategies.

sections.heading: The compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have the necessary
sections.title: Compliance functions must have expertise for risk management
sections.summary: Compliance, risk, and internal audit functions (including IT audit) must have sufficient expertise and resources to provide independent and objective assurance on the cyber and technology risk management framework. This ensures the framework is robust and effectively monitored.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures that internal audit functions can validate the effectiveness of data transfer and processing safeguards.

requirements.text: Compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have necessary expertise
Compliance, risk and internal audit functions shall have necessary resources
Compliance, risk and internal audit functions shall provide independent and objective assurance on cyber and technology risk management framework

sections.source_quote: The compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have the necessary expertise and resources to provide independent and objective assurance on the cyber and technology risk management framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must identify and assess cyber risks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement a framework for identifying cyber and technology risks, document all assets and connections, rate criticality of information assets, maintain an inventory of accounts and services, conduct regular risk assessments, and update these assessments when new products or threats emerge. This ensures comprehensive risk coverage across all systems and third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed identification and assessment of cyber risks, including third-party connections, to ensure secure data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Implement an appropriate framework for identification of cyber and technology risks
Identify, classify and document all functions, roles, processes, assets (including third-party service providers), data, devices, components and connection points
Establish a rating methodology for criticality and sensitivity of individual/system accounts, information assets and systems
Maintain an inventory of individual/system accounts, cyber services, key roles, processes, information assets, third-party service providers and interconnections with criticality ratings
Regularly conduct risk assessments considering critical services, roles, processes (including third parties), information assets and connection points
Conduct risk assessments when new products, services, technologies, projects or connection points are implemented
Conduct risk assessments when new information affecting cyber risks is identified
Maintain up-to-date network system maps including servers, routers, wireless networks, security devices, interconnections, dependencies and data flows
Identify cyber and technology risks from or posed to interconnections and coordinate with relevant entities to improve resilience
Comprehensively document individual and system accounts to track access rights to information assets
Review and update all above regularly and as changes occur to ensure risk assessments remain accurate

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) implement an appropriate framework for the identification of cyber and technology risks; (ii) ensure that all functions, roles, processes, assets (including those involving third-party service providers) and any other data, device(s), component(s) or connection point(s) of the network system are duly identified, classified and documented; (iii) establish a rating methodology for determining the criticality and sensitivity of individual/system accounts, information assets and other information systems; (iv) maintain an inventory of all individual and system accounts (including privileged and remote access accounts), cyber/technology services, key roles, processes, information assets, third-party service providers and interconnections together with criticality rating; (v) regularly conduct risk assessments taking into account critical services, roles, processes (including those involving third-party service providers), information assets and connection points; (vi) conduct risk assessments when new products, services, technologies, projects or connection points are being implemented and when new information affecting the cyber and technology risks, including the threat landscape of the financial institution are identified; (vii) maintain an up-to-date and complete network system (including network resources) maps. The map should, inter alia, contain all servers, routers, wireless networks and security devices forming the network system, interconnections as well as all external connection points, dependencies, and data flows with its connected information assets (including those with third-party service providers); (viii) identify the cyber and technology risks that the institution bears from or poses to its interconnections and coordinate with relevant interconnected entities to, inter alia, work together to address such risks, with the objective of improving the overall resilience of all parties; (ix) comprehensively document all individual and system accounts, including privileged and remote access accounts, so that it can track all access rights to its information assets; and (x) review and update all of the above regularly and as and when changes occur in a timely manner such that the risk assessments remain accurate and up to date.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall, with respect to exchange of information with customers and third
sections.title: Secure customer data exchanges via APIs
sections.summary: Financial institutions must perform risk assessments to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data exchanged with customers and third parties via APIs. They must also implement security features proportional to the sensitivity and business criticality of the data being exchanged.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires security assessments for data exchanges with customers and third parties to ensure secure data transfers.

requirements.text: Perform risk assessment to ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability of data exchanged with customers and third parties via APIs
Implement security features commensurate with sensitivity and business criticality of data being exchanged

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall, with respect to exchange of information with customers and third parties: (i) perform a risk assessment to ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability requirements of data being exchanged are maintained while allowing customers and third parties to connect to its IT systems via APIs; and (ii) ensure implementation of security features commensurate with sensitivity and business criticality of the data being exchanged.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Encouraged automated tools for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to implement automated tools for asset inventory management, identity and access management, and risk monitoring to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of their cyber and technology risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Encourages use of automation to improve the accuracy and timeliness of risk assessments, which can enhance data transfer security.

requirements.text: Implement automated tools (e.g., centralised asset inventory management) to support classification of critical services, roles, processes and information assets
Implement automated tools (e.g., centralised identity and access management) to support identification and classification of roles, user profiles and credentials
Use automated feeds to identify emerging risks and update cyber and technology risk assessment framework

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: (i) implement automated tools (such as a centralised asset inventory management tool) to support the classification of critical services, roles, processes (including those involving third-party service providers), information assets and connection points; (ii) implement automated tools (such as a centralised identity and access management tool) to support the identification and classification of roles, user profiles and individual and system credentials; and (iii) make use of automated feeds in order to identify emerging risks and update its cyber and technology risk assessment framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Security controls for financial systems continuity and data protection
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust security controls ensuring system continuity, availability, confidentiality, integrity and data protection. These controls must address logical, physical, people and third-party security risks while aligning with business goals and risk appetite. Regular assessments are required to maintain effectiveness against evolving cyber threats.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to maintain security controls that ensure system continuity and data protection, directly impacting cross-border data transfers through security requirements for information systems.

requirements.text: Implement robust security controls ensuring system continuity and availability
Ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability of information stored in systems
Develop security controls addressing logical, physical, people and third-party security
Ensure security controls are commensurate with cyber risks and consistent with business goals
Regularly assess security controls for effectiveness across all assets
Ensure security controls remain relevant to evolving cyber threats
Consider international standards and best practices when developing security controls
Integrate security checks into systems from the earliest development stages
Regularly review information security management system through certification, audits or testing

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: put in place a robust and effective set of security controls that will enable it to fulfil its security objectives, which should include ensuring: the continuity and availability of its information systems; and the confidentiality, integrity and availability of information stored in its information systems

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Network security boundaries and segmentation for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish secure network boundaries using firewalls, routers and virtual private networks. They must implement network segmentation based on system sensitivity, define baseline security requirements, use encrypted protocols, ensure end-to-end encryption for electronic channels, and maintain network diagrams that describe hardware, applications and data flows. Regular scanning for rogue devices and access points is required.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires secure network boundaries and segmentation that impact data transfer security, particularly for cross-border electronic communications and data flows.

requirements.text: Set up secure network boundaries using routers, firewalls or virtual private networks
Distinguish trusted and untrusted areas based on risk profile and criticality
Apply reasonable access criteria using least privilege principle
Implement network segmentation according to system sensitivity
Define baseline systems and security requirements for remote access
Use secured network protocols for confidentiality and integrity of information
Establish end-to-end encrypted connections between branches, customer devices and systems
Provide reliability, redundancy and non-repudiation for online financial systems
Limit, lock and terminate system sessions after inactivity period
Maintain up-to-date network and data flow diagrams
Implement technical measures to prevent unauthorized code execution
Strictly control and monitor system configuration changes
Detect and block actual and attempted attacks or intrusions
Prevent non-controlled devices from connecting to internal networks
Log and monitor network activities for inappropriate use or access attempts
Scan networks regularly to detect rogue devices and access points
Prevent users from installing unauthorized applications
Segment network infrastructure with adequate security policies

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: set up a secure boundary to protect its network infrastructure through the use of technologies such as, inter alia, a router, firewall or virtual private network.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Access control and privilege management for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must restrict logical access to system resources based on need to know and least privilege. They must establish policies for access privileges that are promptly revoked upon employment changes and reviewed annually. Strong security standards for APIs include key management, encryption, documentation of third-party access and revocation procedures. Dual control for critical systems is required, with regular reviews of access privileges and monitoring for anomalous behavior.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict access controls and privilege management that directly impact data transfer security and cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Identify and restrict logical access to system resources based on need to know
Establish policies for access privileges that are promptly revoked upon employment changes
Review access privileges at least annually
Implement comprehensive key management lifecycle for API access tokens
Ensure strong encryption standards for API data transmission
Document third-party access through APIs including identity, data accessed and timing
Revoke API keys or access tokens upon suspicious activity detection
Establish dual control for access to critical systems
Regularly review access to detect unnecessary privileges
Block unauthorized access to systems
Limit and monitor access to critical systems with audit trails
Restrict system administration privileges to operational requirements
Track privileged users' actions to detect anomalous behavior
Implement controls to prevent unauthorized privilege escalation
Ensure encryption of data at rest and in transit for critical data
Promptly revoke access rights of departing employees
Require departing employees to return all information assets
Adjust access rights upon employment status changes
Enforce strong password controls including one-time passwords
Implement minimum password length and alphanumeric requirements
Establish criteria for password history and common passwords
Require regular password changes
Implement multi-factor authentication for higher risk access

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: identify and restrict logical access to its system resources to the minimum required for legitimate and approved work activities, according to the principle of need to know and least privilege

sections.heading: A financial institution shall establish an appropriate framework in respect of multi-factor
sections.title: Multi-factor authentication for high-risk transactions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish multi-factor authentication frameworks for electronic delivery channels that consider transaction risk, value and recurrence. Higher risk transactions require multi-factor authentication to enhance security for sensitive financial activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires multi-factor authentication for high-risk transactions, directly impacting data transfer security for financial transactions across borders.

requirements.text: Establish multi-factor authentication framework for electronic delivery channels
Consider transaction risk, value and recurrence when implementing multi-factor authentication
Apply multi-factor authentication to higher risk transactions

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall establish an appropriate framework in respect of multi-factor authentication for electronic delivery channels. This shall amongst others take into consideration the nature and the level of risk involved in the transaction, the value of transaction and the recurrence of the transaction. Higher risk transactions shall be subject to multi-factor authentication.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Encouraged access management automation
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to automate access account administration to disable temporary accounts, implement automated identity and access management tools for system updates, and establish automatic notifications for access changes upon employment status changes. One-time passwords for critical applications are also encouraged.
sections.dtia_summary: Encouraged automation of access management processes that could enhance data transfer security for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Automate administration of information system access accounts to disable temporary accounts
Implement automated identity and access management tools for system updates
Establish automatic notifications for access changes upon employment status changes
Use one-time passwords for critical applications

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: automate the administration of information system access accounts to, inter alia, disable and/or delete disabled, temporary and emergency accounts after a predetermined period of time and to detect unauthorised access

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall:
sections.title: Data encryption and cryptographic controls
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish encryption requirements aligned with recognized standards, implement controls to prevent unauthorized access to encryption keys, and manage cryptographic materials through hardware security modules, virtual tools or third-party providers. They must ensure secure key storage separate from data and maintain appropriate segregation of access to keys and customer information.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strong encryption and cryptographic controls that directly impact data transfer security and cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Establish encryption requirements aligned with recognized standards
Ensure encryption level considers criticality of underlying data
Store encryption keys separately from virtual images and data
Implement controls to prevent unauthorized access to encryption keys
Specify separate policies for management and access to cryptographic materials
Use hardware security modules, virtual tools or third-party providers for cryptography
Ensure third-party providers have arrangements to secure cryptographic materials
Understand third-party provider access to keys and cryptographic materials
Ensure segregation between key access and customer information access
Control access to cryptographic keys for core banking services

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall: establish requirements with regard to data encryption and use encryption and general cryptographic controls in accordance with recognised standards and processes, which address issues such as algorithm, key length, and key generation, among others.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Physical security for financial systems and data
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement physical security measures to protect premises, sensitive areas, and data centers from unauthorized access and hazards. They must restrict physical access to information systems based on need-to-know and least privilege principles. Institutions must maintain access logs, ensure critical systems have additional physical controls like CCTV and alarms, cancel access rights upon employee changes, and maintain technology asset inventories with criticality ratings and locations.
sections.dtia_summary: Physical security requirements directly impact data center and infrastructure safeguards, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border data transfers. These measures ensure physical protection of data assets during transfer and storage.

requirements.text: Implement appropriate measures to protect premises, sensitive areas and data centres from unauthorised access and natural hazards
Restrict physical access to information systems to minimum required for legitimate work activities based on need to know and least privilege
Maintain logs to identify physical access to information systems
Ensure limited physical access to critical systems with additional controls like CCTV monitoring and alarms
Cancel or adjust access rights promptly upon change in employee status
Maintain inventory of technology assets including criticality rating and physical location

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) implement appropriate measures to protect its premises, sensitive areas and data centres from unauthorised access and from natural and other hazards; (ii) identify and restrict physical access to information systems to the minimum required for legitimate and approved work activities, according to the principle of need to know and least privilege; (iii) ensure that appropriate logs are maintained to identify physical access to information systems; (iv) ensure that there is limited physical access to critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets and that same are duly logged. Critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets should also have additional physical controls such as being monitored by CCTV cameras and being secured by an alarm. The monitoring and management of CCTV cameras should be outside the control of the IT department; (v) ensure that access rights are promptly cancelled or adjusted upon change in employment status of employees; and (vi) maintain an inventory of their technology assets including their criticality rating, physical location (for instance, servers and their respective locations).

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Change and patch management for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish change management policies with prioritization criteria, ensure backups and rollback strategies for changes, implement patch management processes for security and functional updates, test changes before deployment, specify emergency modification review and authorization processes, and maintain recovery plans for information systems.
sections.dtia_summary: Change and patch management requirements are critical for DTIAs as they affect the security and stability of data during transfer and storage, particularly for systems handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: Have policies, procedures and controls for change management including criteria for prioritising and classifying changes
Ensure all related information is backed up and a roll-back strategy is created before implementing changes
Establish a patch management process to enforce relevant functional and non-functional patches within a period commensurate with criticality
Ensure patches and changes are thoroughly tested before deployment
Specify processes for reviewing, authorising and enforcing emergency modifications
Have appropriate recovery plans in place

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) have policies, procedures and controls in place for change management, including criteria for prioritising and classifying the changes; (ii) ensure that all related information is backed up and a roll-back strategy is created before implementing changes to information assets to ensure that they are evaluated, checked, reviewed and accepted; (iii) establish a patch management process to ensure that relevant functional and non-functional patches (e.g. corrections for security vulnerabilities and program bugs) are enforced within a period commensurate with the patches' criticality and the financial institution's information systems; (iv) ensure that patches and changes are thoroughly tested before deployment; (v) specify the processes for reviewing, authorising, and enforcing emergency modifications; and (vi) have in place appropriate recovery plans.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to consider using standardised configuration of IT
sections.title: Standardized IT configuration for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to use standardized IT configurations to facilitate their patch management processes, which helps improve security and efficiency in managing technology assets.
sections.dtia_summary: Standardized IT configurations can enhance data transfer security by reducing vulnerabilities and ensuring consistent patch management practices across systems.

requirements.text: Consider using standardized configuration of IT resources to facilitate patch management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to consider using standardised configuration of IT resources to facilitate its patch management process.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Technology refresh for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must avoid outdated and unsupported hardware/software, develop refresh plans for replacing end-of-support technologies, conduct risk evaluations for continued use, and regularly scan outdated systems for vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Technology refresh requirements ensure that systems handling cross-border data are up-to-date and secure, reducing risks associated with data transfers and storage.

requirements.text: Avoid using outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies
Develop a technology refresh plan for replacing outdated and unsupported hardware or software
Ensure risk evaluation for outdated and unsupported hardware and software to determine risk and appropriate risk reduction mechanisms
Ensure outdated and unsupported hardware and software are regularly scanned for potential vulnerabilities

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) avoid using outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies; (ii) develop a technology refresh plan for replacing outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies, that are reaching end of support (EOS); (iii) ensure that a risk evaluation for outdated and unsupported hardware and software, including legacy technologies, is duly undertaken to determine the risk of continuing to use them and the appropriate risk reduction mechanisms for maintaining the risk at an acceptable level. The risk evaluation and risk reduction plan should be duly approved by the board or the designated sub-committee of the board and senior management; and (iv) ensure that outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies are regularly scanned to identify potential vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that:
sections.title: Staff competence and security for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent to handle technology risks, conduct background checks and fit and proper tests commensurate with data criticality, and monitor physical and logical behavior patterns for anomalous activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Staff security requirements directly impact DTIAs by ensuring personnel handling cross-border data are vetted and monitored for unauthorized access or breaches.

requirements.text: Ensure all staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent and skilled enough to conduct assigned roles and handle technology risks
Conduct appropriate background checks and fit and proper tests on staff, including contractors and service providers, commensurate with criticality of data and systems
Monitor physical and logical behavior patterns for critical information assets/systems to identify anomalous activities

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that: (i) all staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent and skilled enough to conduct the assigned roles and handle technology risks; (ii) appropriate background checks and fit and proper tests are conducted on staff, including contractors and service providers and their staff, commensurate with the criticality of the data and information systems to which they would have access; and (iii) physical and logical behaviour patterns (e.g. network use patterns and work hours, etc.) are monitored for critical information assets/systems to identify anomalous activities.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics,
sections.title: Innovative threat detection for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to implement real-time threat detection solutions like data analytics, machine learning, and artificial intelligence to identify and respond to security threats.
sections.dtia_summary: Real-time threat detection capabilities enhance DTIAs by enabling faster identification and response to security incidents during data transfers and storage.

requirements.text: Implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics, machine learning and artificial intelligence) to detect and respond to threat activity in real time

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics, machine learning and artificial intelligence, etc.) to detect and respond to threat activity in real time.

sections.heading: The extent of due diligence to be performed, including the requirements for onsite audits or
sections.title: Due diligence scope based on service materiality and reliance level
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct due diligence on third-party service providers proportional to the materiality of services and IT assets, and the level of reliance on the provider for security controls. The scope must consider the provider's risk management systems, security capabilities, and vulnerability assessments. This applies to all third-party service providers handling financial institution data.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must assess the proportionality of due diligence based on service materiality and institutional reliance to ensure adequate security controls for cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: Due diligence scope must be commensurate with service materiality and IT asset importance
Due diligence must consider level of reliance on third-party provider for security controls

sections.source_quote: The extent of due diligence to be performed, including the requirements for onsite audits or remote audits, shall be commensurate with materiality of the services and of the IT assets involved and the level of reliance the financial institution places on the third-party service provider to maintain effective security controls.

sections.heading: The due diligence on a third-party service provider in respect of material services shall, inter
sections.title: Due diligence components for material third-party service providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must evaluate third-party service providers for material services through specific criteria including risk management systems, compliance with regulations, financial resilience, personnel screening, recovery capabilities, and track record. This assessment must be conducted before onboarding and periodically to ensure ongoing compliance with security and data protection standards.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party service providers meet specific security and compliance criteria before data transfers, ensuring that critical data handling meets the required resilience and security standards.

requirements.text: Evaluate adequacy of third-party service provider's risk management and internal control systems
Assess third-party service provider's compliance with data protection and information security regulations
Verify third-party service provider's ability to maintain services during adverse conditions like cyberattacks
Confirm third-party service provider's ability to recover systems within stipulated recovery time objectives
Ensure personnel with access to customer information undergo adequate background screening
Assess financial and operational resilience of third-party service providers
Evaluate third-party service provider's track record over at least three years

sections.source_quote: The due diligence on a third-party service provider in respect of material services shall, inter alia, include: the adequacy of the third-party service provider's risk management and internal control systems, information security capabilities and security controls including the controls for protecting the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data taking into consideration the findings of vulnerabilities assessment, penetration testing, audit and/or other reviews provided by the third-party service provider, where relevant; the third-party service provider's compliance with the requirements of this Guideline, the applicable data protection, confidentiality and information security regulations or other legislations and adherence to international IT standards; the willingness and ability of the third-party service provider to service commitments even under adverse conditions, for instance, in the event of a cyber-attack or data theft; the ability of the third-party service providers to recover outsourced systems and IT services within the stipulated recovery time objective and recovery point objective; the verification of whether the personnel of the third-party service provider (including employees and subcontractors) with access to customer information are subject to adequate background screening, security training, access approvals and confidentiality arrangements; forward looking assessment of the financial and operational resilience of the third-party service provider; and an assessment of the proven track record of at least three years of the third-party service provider for such services.

sections.heading: With respect to third-party service providers having access to its information assets or hosting
sections.title: Obligations for third-party service providers with access to information assets
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure third-party service providers comply with all guideline requirements, undergo due diligence before onboarding, maintain updated inventories of critical third parties, and include specific contractual provisions such as compliance with data protection laws, confidentiality obligations, record-keeping requirements, and exit strategies. These obligations apply to all third-party service providers handling financial institution information assets.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must ensure contractual clauses for third-party service providers include robust compliance mechanisms, data protection safeguards, and clear exit strategies to mitigate risks associated with cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Ensure third-party service provider complies with all guideline requirements
Conduct due diligence before onboarding third-party service providers
Maintain updated inventory of third-party service providers with criticality ratings
Ensure contracts do not hinder Bank's supervisory powers
Include clauses ensuring continued effectiveness of cyber and technology risk management framework
Include clauses ensuring compliance with relevant Mauritian data protection laws
Include confidentiality obligations aligned with Banking Act 2004
Include clauses ensuring compliance with Banking Act 2004 record-keeping obligations
Include obligation for third-party service provider to provide notice of subcontractor or location changes
Grant right of audit by Bank, financial institution, or external auditor
Grant right of access to audit reports by Bank or external auditor
Grant right to take possession of services and data during license revocation
Allow financial institution to terminate agreements based on concerns about third-party service provider
Require third-party service provider to cooperate with Bank and provide access to information
Include comprehensive exit strategies with regular reviews

sections.source_quote: With respect to third-party service providers having access to its information assets or hosting its information assets, a financial institution shall: ensure that the third-party service provider is complying with all requirements of the guideline; ensure that the necessary due diligence is carried out before onboarding the third-party service provider; be aware of the information security measures and controls established and ensure that they are in line with the requirements of the guideline; maintain an updated inventory of third-party service providers which have access to its information assets together with their criticality rating; ensure that the contracts with third-party service providers are duly executed, do not consist of any clause that would hinder the Bank from exercising its supervisory powers, and include relevant provisions: to ensure the continued effectiveness of its cyber and technology risk management framework; to ensure compliance with relevant laws in Mauritius (e.g. The Cybersecurity and Cybercrime Act 2021, Data Protection Act 2017, ICTA, etc.) or to relevant and equivalent laws at all times; to impose confidentiality obligations on the third-party service provider which are in line with the underlying objective of section 64 of the Banking Act 2004 or section 18 of the National Payment Systems Act 2018, as relevant; to ensure compliance with the record keeping obligations as set out in the Banking Act 2004; on the right of audit (including remote audit) by the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditor, or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank, the financial institution or its external auditor. The cost of audit by any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank shall be borne by the financial institution; on the right of access by the Bank, the financial institution or its external auditor to relevant audit reports/reports of other tests conducted by or arranged by the third-party service providers; on the right of the Bank or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank to promptly take possession of all services and data relating to the financial institution in the event the Bank decides to revoke the licence of the financial institution or appoints a conservator. The procedures for executing the change of ownership request and for ensuring continuity of services during this process should be duly documented and agreed with the third-party service provider; on the right of the financial institution to terminate the agreement where, inter alia, the financial institution has concerns on the third-party service provider, the location of the data and the subcontractors involved; to ensure the obligation of the third-party service provider, where applicable, to cooperate with the Bank and provide access to information required by the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditor, or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank; on exit strategies which are comprehensive and well documented. The exit strategies shall be regularly reviewed to ensure that they remain adequate and effective. The exit plan shall, as a minimum, include the following: incident management process, including the roles and responsibilities of each party; the governing law; and dispute resolution considering the chosen governing law for the contract;

sections.heading: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant
sections.title: Permanent data irrecoverability assurance
sections.summary: Financial institutions must obtain assurance from third-party service providers through independent reports or certificates that all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible within a timely period after contract termination. This requirement applies to all third-party service providers handling financial institution data.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party service providers can securely delete data upon contract termination to prevent residual data exposure in cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Obtain assurance from third-party service provider through independent reports/certificates
Ensure all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible after contract termination

sections.source_quote: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant independent reports/certificates that all data of the financial institution (including any backup) is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible in a timely manner after termination of the contract;

sections.heading: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity
sections.title: Business continuity alternative solutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify alternative solutions to maintain business continuity throughout and after the transition phase when outsourcing services. This includes assessing risks, vulnerabilities, criticality of assets, impact of process changes, and ensuring data segregation and encryption for customer information.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must evaluate alternative solutions for business continuity to ensure uninterrupted data processing and transfer capabilities during third-party transitions.

requirements.text: Identify alternative solutions for business continuity throughout and after transition phase
Assess cyber and technology resilience capabilities of third-party service providers
Identify associated risks including cyber/IT related risks and concentration risks
Evaluate vulnerabilities, benefits, and sustainability of services
Evaluate impact of changes to processes and procedures
Evaluate adequacy of internal cyber/technology risk management framework
Ensure data, including customer information, is logically segregated and encrypted
Identify roles and accountabilities for financial institution and third-party service provider
Assess adequacy of control framework
Evaluate impact of possible risk events including third-party failure
Assess adequacy of contingency and exit plans including data portability
Evaluate risk of foreign authorities accessing data
Ensure compliance with relevant legal and regulatory requirements
Ensure third-party service provider complies with guideline requirements
Assess third-party service provider's track record

sections.source_quote: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity throughout and after the transition phase;

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall ensure that third-party service providers involve in the conduct of
sections.title: Accredited third-party testing providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that third-party service providers conducting independent audits, vulnerability assessments, penetration testing, and other testing exercises are accredited by a reputable body. This requirement applies to all third-party service providers handling financial institution data.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party service providers have accredited testing capabilities to ensure robust security assessments for data transfers.

requirements.text: Ensure third-party service providers conducting independent audits are accredited by reputable body
Ensure third-party service providers conducting vulnerability assessments are accredited by reputable body
Ensure third-party service providers conducting penetration testing are accredited by reputable body

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall ensure that third-party service providers involve in the conduct of independent audits, vulnerability assessments, penetration testing and other testing exercises are duly accredited by a reputed body.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to automate the process for detection and prevention of
sections.title: Automate detection of unauthorized third-party access
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to automate processes for detecting and preventing unauthorized access from third-party service providers. This encourages proactive security measures to protect against breaches originating from external service providers. The obligation applies to financial institutions handling customer data through third-party services.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to implement automated security monitoring for third-party access, which impacts data transfer and storage security assessments when using external service providers.

requirements.text: Automate the process for detection and prevention of unauthorized access from third-party service providers

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to automate the process for detection and prevention of unauthorized access from third-party service providers.

sections.heading: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for the conduct of external independent
sections.title: Third-party service provider responsibility duration limit
sections.summary: Third-party service providers cannot be responsible for external independent assessments, penetration testing, and vulnerability assessments for more than three consecutive years. This section sets a maximum duration for third-party security responsibilities to ensure ongoing security practices are maintained by qualified entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Limits the period third-party providers can handle security assessments, affecting how financial institutions manage third-party security responsibilities and data transfer risk assessments.

requirements.text: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for more than 3 years

sections.source_quote: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for the conduct of external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for a continuous period of more than 3 years.

sections.heading: Where a third-party service provider has been responsible for the conduct of external
sections.title: Third-party service provider re-engagement period
sections.summary: After a third-party service provider has conducted security assessments for three years or less, they must wait two years before being entrusted with similar tasks again. This ensures a cooling-off period to maintain security standards and prevent potential risks from repeated engagements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a two-year waiting period after third-party security assessments to prevent immediate re-engagement, impacting data transfer and security assessments for third-party providers.

requirements.text: Where a third-party service provider has conducted external independent assessments for 3 years or less, they must wait 2 years before being entrusted with similar tasks again

sections.source_quote: Where a third-party service provider has been responsible for the conduct of external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for a continuous period of 3 years or less, that firm shall not be entrusted the responsibility of the aforementioned tasks before a period of 2 years from its last assignment.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall be aware of the location (city and country) where their customer
sections.title: Customer data hosting location compliance
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify where customer data is hosted globally, and ensure that foreign jurisdictions hosting data comply with Mauritian data protection laws. This includes verifying that foreign authorities cannot access data, contractual provisions allow termination for location changes, and data protection laws in foreign jurisdictions are equivalent to Mauritian standards. The institution must also ensure service providers notify them before disclosing data to foreign authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to assess foreign hosting locations for customer data, ensuring compliance with Mauritian data protection laws and preventing unauthorized foreign access, critical for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must identify the city and country where customer data is hosted
Service providers must advise on past and potential disclosures of client data to foreign authorities
Governing law must ensure enforceability of contractual provisions in case of breach
Foreign jurisdiction data protection laws must align with Mauritian data protection laws
Foreign jurisdictions must not restrict on-site audits or access rights for financial institutions
