Foreign jurisdictions must not restrict access to data by financial institutions
Service providers must allow financial institutions to terminate agreements if location changes cause concerns
Service providers must notify financial institutions before disclosing data to foreign authorities
Service providers must implement legal and protective measures if prior notice is legally prohibited
Financial institutions must be informed of any disclosures made by service providers to foreign authorities

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall be aware of the location (city and country) where their customer and other material information assets/systems will be hosted and shall ensure that: [list of conditions]

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall ensure that the due diligence under sections 43 and 44 are conducted
sections.title: Due diligence for data hosting compliance
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that due diligence for data hosting compliance is conducted by a competent officer or reputable firm. This ensures that assessments of data storage locations and security practices are carried out by qualified personnel to maintain robust data protection standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to have qualified personnel or firms conduct due diligence on data hosting locations, impacting the assessment of third-party data storage risks.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must ensure due diligence under sections 43 and 44 is conducted by a competent officer or reputable firm

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall ensure that the due diligence under sections 43 and 44 are conducted by a competent officer of the financial institution or a reputed firm, as deemed appropriate.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall establish a pre-agreed list of locations where its data will be
sections.title: Pre-agreed data processing locations
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a pre-agreed list of locations where data will be processed with service providers. If data is processed outside this list, it must be limited to individual transactions, promptly informed about the location, and ensured to be securely removed and transferred to a pre-agreed location within a reasonable timeframe.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to maintain a pre-agreed list of data processing locations, critical for assessing data storage risks when using third-party service providers across borders.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must establish a pre-agreed list of locations where data will be processed with service providers
Data processed outside the pre-agreed list must involve only individual transactions initiated by end users
Financial institutions must be promptly informed by service providers of data processing locations and rationale
Service providers must ensure data is processed in a secured environment and permanently removed from the location

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall establish a pre-agreed list of locations where its data will be processed with the service provider. Where data is processed in a location outside the pre-agreed list, the financial institution shall ensure that: [list of conditions]

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall implement secure coding, source code review and application
sections.title: Secure coding and application security testing for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement secure coding practices, source code reviews, and application security testing. Critical applications must comply with relevant international standards. These requirements apply to all financial institutions handling digital systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific security controls for critical applications, directly impacting how financial institutions assess and implement technical safeguards during data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall implement secure coding
Financial institutions shall implement source code review
Financial institutions shall implement application security testing
For critical applications, financial institutions shall abide with relevant international standards

sections.source_quote: 47. Financial institutions shall implement secure coding, source code review and application security testing. For critical applications, financial institutions shall abide with relevant international standards.

sections.heading: The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass such areas as secure
sections.title: Secure coding standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must define secure coding standards that cover secure programming techniques, input validation, output encoding, access control, authentication, encryption, and error handling. These standards apply to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies technical security requirements that must be integrated into software development processes, affecting how data is handled and transferred across systems.

requirements.text: The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass secure programming techniques
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass input validation
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass output encoding
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass access control
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass authentication
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass encryption
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass errors and exceptions

sections.source_quote: 48. The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass such areas as secure programming techniques, input validation, output encoding, access control, authentication, encryption, errors and exceptions.

sections.heading: A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should
sections.title: Policy for open-source and third-party software usage
sections.summary: Financial institutions must develop a policy and procedure for using open-source and third-party software that requires these codes to be reviewed and tested before integration into their systems. This policy applies to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific security controls for software integration, directly impacting how financial institutions manage third-party data sources and transfer risks.

requirements.text: A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should be developed
The policy shall require that these codes are reviewed and tested before integration into the software/system of the financial institution

sections.source_quote: 49. A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should be developed. The policy shall, inter alia, require that these codes are reviewed and tested before integration into the software/system of the financial institution.

sections.heading: The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the
sections.title: Qualified software developers for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure their software developers have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications. This requirement applies to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that developers are competent in security practices, which is critical for maintaining secure data handling and transfer processes.

requirements.text: The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications

sections.source_quote: 50. The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before
sections.title: Risk management framework for end-user applications
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement a risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users that are not maintained by their IT department. This framework must include adequate testing and approval processes for such applications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific risk management controls for end-user applications, which directly impacts how financial institutions assess and manage data transfer risks from non-IT-developed applications.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users and which are not maintained by its IT department
This framework shall include adequate testing and approval

sections.source_quote: 51. Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users and which are not maintained by its IT department. This shall, among others, include adequate testing and approval.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Cyber and technology incident detection requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must monitor internal and external variables using signature and behavior-based detection mechanisms, establish baselines for user/device activities, actively monitor for phishing campaigns, implement real-time transaction monitoring, notify customers for high-risk transactions, track user activity, and ensure regular review of detection capabilities. These requirements apply to all financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates comprehensive incident detection mechanisms that directly impact how financial institutions identify and respond to data breaches and security incidents during data transfers.

requirements.text: Monitor internal and external variables by using a combination of signature monitoring for known vulnerabilities and behaviourally based detection mechanisms
Identify, consider and record the baseline profile of user/device activities based on the risk assessment conducted during the identification process to aid in detecting deviation from the baseline
Actively monitor for phishing campaigns targeting the financial institution or its customers
Establish a real-time monitoring system to detect suspicious or fraudulent transactions
Establish a system for notification of transactions above a certain limit and of higher risk transactions to their customers, including relevant details of the transaction
Monitor user activity, exceptions, cyber and technology risk events, connections, external service providers, devices and software and analyse same for unusual behaviour
Ensure that the effectiveness and appropriateness of its detection capabilities are regularly reviewed and enhanced and that related staff receive relevant training on the matter
Identify warning signs for detecting cyber and technology incidents and for determining if breaches have occurred

Employ a defence-in-depth strategy by implementing multi-layered detection controls that cover individuals, procedures, and technologies, with each layer acting as a safety net for the layers before it
Be able to track cyber and technology incidents and quickly change its security controls, working in cooperation with other stakeholders

sections.source_quote: 52. A financial institution shall: (i) monitor internal and external variables by using a combination of signature monitoring for known vulnerabilities and behaviourally based detection mechanisms; (ii) identify, consider and record the baseline profile of user/device activities based on the risk assessment conducted during the identification process to aid in detecting deviation from the baseline such as anomalous activities and events; (iii) actively monitor for phishing campaigns targeting the financial institution or its customers; (iv) with regard to electronic delivery channels: a. establish a real-time monitoring system to detect suspicious or fraudulent transactions; and b. establish a system for notification of transactions above a certain limit and of higher risk transactions to their customers, including relevant details of the transaction such as type of transaction, amount of the transaction and timing of the transaction; (v) monitor user activity, exceptions, cyber and technology risk events, connections, external service providers, devices and software and analyse same for unusual behaviour; (vi) ensure that the effectiveness and appropriateness of its detection capabilities are regularly reviewed and enhanced and that related staff receive relevant training on the matter; (vii) identify warning signs for detecting cyber and technology incidents and for determining if breaches have occurred; (viii) employ a defence-in-depth strategy by implementing multi-layered detection controls that cover individuals, procedures, and technologies, with each layer acting as a safety net for the layers before it; and (ix) be able to track cyber and technology incidents and quickly change its security controls, working in cooperation with other stakeholders.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Establish security operations center and event monitoring systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a Security Operations Centre or equivalent to monitor anomalous activities and events. They must also design automated systems like SIEM to centralize and analyze event information across sources. These systems must continuously track the risk environment to identify potential threats and vulnerabilities in real-time.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires robust monitoring and event analysis capabilities to detect threats early, which is critical for assessing cross-border data transfer risks in financial systems.

requirements.text: Set up a Security Operations Centre or equivalent for tracking and detecting anomalous activities and events
Design and implement automated systems (e.g. SIEM) to capture, centralise, and compare event information from various sources
Log analysis to constantly track the risk environment

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: (i) set up a Security Operations Centre or equivalent for tracking and detecting anomalous activities and events; and (ii) design and implement automated systems (e.g. a security information and event management (SIEM) system) and/or relevant processes to capture, centralise, and compare event information from various sources, as well as log analysis, to constantly track the risk environment.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall have a board approved business continuity plan which, as a
sections.title: Board-approved business continuity plan with third-party resilience
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved business continuity plan that identifies critical services, recovery objectives, and scenarios for cyber incidents. The plan must include business impact analyses, recovery strategies, third-party provider assessments, disaster recovery sites, and annual reviews to adapt to evolving threats. It must ensure third-party service providers meet resilience standards through testing and certification.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates comprehensive business continuity planning that directly impacts data storage and transfer resilience across borders, especially for critical financial systems.

requirements.text: Identify critical services, key roles, processes, information assets and third-party service providers
Define recovery point objectives and recovery time objectives
Consider extreme but plausible scenarios of cyber and technology incidents
Include business impact analyses to assess potential impact quantitatively and qualitatively
Set out recovery and response plans dependent on scenarios to safeguard critical assets
Ensure recovery and response team has necessary skills, experience and training
Define parameters for detecting cyber incidents triggering recovery plans
Ensure third-party service providers' response and recovery plans are satisfactory
Consider continuity measures to mitigate third-party service provider failures
Develop, document and agree with third-party service providers on disaster recovery plans
Ensure third-party service providers' business continuity plans are regularly tested
Ensure third-party service providers have system resiliency and network redundancy
Set up disaster recovery sites geographically separated from primary sites
Ensure dual connectivity between main premises, disaster recovery sites and alternate data centres
Conduct ex-post investigation of cyber incidents and integrate findings into risk management framework
Review scenarios and business impact analyses annually to account for changes

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall have a board approved business continuity plan which, as a minimum: (i) identifies its critical services, key roles, processes, information assets and third-party service providers; (ii) defines its recovery point objectives and recovery time objectives; (iii) considers extreme but plausible scenarios of cyber and technology incidents or other incidents which would impact its systems; (iv) includes business impact analyses to assess the potential impact of those scenarios quantitatively and qualitatively; (v) sets out recovery and response plans, dependent on the various scenarios, to safeguard critical assets, maintain critical services and achieve its recovery objectives; (vi) ensures that its recovery and response team have the necessary skills, experience and training to address cyber or technology incidents; (vii) defines parameters regarding detection of cyber or technology incidents which would trigger its recovery and response plans; (viii) ensures that the response and recovery plans of third-party service providers are satisfactory; (ix) considers continuity measures to mitigate the impact of failures of third-party service providers.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall have a board approved response and recovery plan for cyber and
sections.title: Board-approved cyber incident response and recovery plan
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved response and recovery plan for cyber incidents that identifies key stakeholders, defines roles and responsibilities, focuses on critical business operations, sets escalation criteria, ensures thorough incident investigations, and outlines immediate containment actions. The plan must also specify digital evidence sharing protocols with relevant authorities and stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific incident response protocols that are critical for assessing data transfer risks during cyber incidents, particularly for cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: Identify staff and external stakeholders essential to deal with cyber incidents
Set out policies, procedures, roles and responsibilities to respond to and recover from cyber incidents
Focus on recovery of critical business operations to avoid adverse effects on financial systems
Set criteria for prompt escalation of cyber incidents to the board or senior management
Conduct thorough investigation to determine incident nature and damage extent
Take immediate actions to prevent further damage and contain cyber incidents
Identify affected systems, carry out malware analysis and check for attacker response
Include details of internal and external stakeholders, information to share and reporting timeframes

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall have a board approved response and recovery plan for cyber and technology incidents which, as a minimum: (i) identifies staff and other external stakeholders who are essential to deal with such incidents and who would be alerted in case such an incident is detected; (ii) sets out the policies, procedures, roles and responsibilities to respond to and recover from cyber and technology incidents; (iii) focuses on recovery of critical business operations, supporting processes, information assets and their interdependencies to avoid adverse effects on its operations and on the financial system, including on payment systems and on payment service users, and to ensure execution of pending payment transactions; (iv) sets out the criteria for prompt escalation of cyber or technology incidents to the board or senior management based on the risk posed by the incident; (v) ensures that, upon detection of an incident, a thorough investigation is conducted to determine the nature of the incident and the extent of the damage caused by the incident; (vi) sets out the immediate actions to be taken to prevent further damage and to contain a cyber or technology incident; (vii) ensures that, after the cyber or technology incident is contained, key components of the incident are eliminated through inter alia, identifying all affected systems in the financial institution so that they can be remediated, carrying out malware analysis and checking for any response from the attacker; and (viii) includes details of internal and external stakeholders, including the Bank, the information that has to be shared and reported together with relevant timeframes, including in case of emergency.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Secure backup storage and restoration procedures
sections.summary: Financial institutions must store backup information securely at alternate sites both online and offline. They must establish backup strategies based on information criticality, ensure rapid restoration with minimal downtime, test restoration procedures at least twice a year for critical information, and protect backup data confidentiality, integrity and availability during storage and transmission.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires secure backup and restoration protocols that are essential for assessing data transfer risks when systems are disrupted, particularly for financial data.

requirements.text: Store backup information at an alternate site, both online and offline, safeguarded by protective and detective controls
Establish a system and data backup strategy with appropriate frequency based on criticality
Ensure restoration of system and data backups can be carried out with minimum downtime
Test restoration of system and data backups at least twice a year for critical information
Ensure backup data is protected at rest and in transit for confidentiality, integrity and availability

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) store backup information at an alternate site, both online and offline, which should be safeguarded by protective and detective controls; (ii) establish a system and data backup strategy with appropriate frequency of backup which is based on the criticality of the information; (iii) ensure that the restoration of its system and data backups can be carried out with minimum downtime and limited disruption, in line with recovery objectives; (iv) test the restoration of its system and data backups to validate the effectiveness of its backup restoration procedures. The frequency shall be commensurate to the criticality of the information and be at least twice a year for critical information and systems; and (v) ensure that any backup data is protected at rest and in transit to ensure its confidentiality, integrity and availability.

sections.heading: With a view to facilitating forensic investigation, a financial institution shall:
sections.title: Forensic readiness for digital evidence
sections.summary: Financial institutions must consider threat scenarios to determine required digital evidence, securely collect and store logs, retain critical logs for at least 7 years, train staff on evidence handling, ensure forensic expertise, and periodically review forensic readiness procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates forensic readiness protocols that are critical for assessing data transfer risks during incidents, particularly for cross-border data investigations.

requirements.text: Consider different threat scenarios and determine types of logs and digital evidence to collect
Ensure logs and digital evidence are collected, handled and stored securely
Develop internal policy for retention of digital evidence with minimum 7-year storage for critical logs
Train staff on responsibilities related to handling digital evidence
Ensure staff designated for forensic investigations have necessary knowledge and expertise
Periodically review forensic readiness procedures based on new knowledge and experience

sections.source_quote: With a view to facilitating forensic investigation, a financial institution shall: (i) consider different threat scenarios and determine the types of logs and other pieces of digital evidence that should be collected; (ii) ensure that the logs and other digital evidence are collected, handled and stored securely and are duly backed up; (iii) develop an internal policy for the retention of digital evidence and logs. As a minimum, critical logs and digital evidence shall be stored for a minimum of 7 years; (iv) train its staff such that all those involved in an incident understand their responsibilities related to handling of digital evidence; (v) ensure that staff specifically designated to conduct forensic investigation have the necessary knowledge and expertise to handle the digital evidence; and (vi) periodically review its forensic readiness procedures in light of new knowledge and experience.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall test the adequacy of its information security controls through well-
sections.title: Regular testing of information security controls
sections.summary: Financial institutions must test the adequacy of their information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programs. The frequency and scope of these tests must align with evolving threats, information asset criticality, incident consequences, policy enforcement risks, and changes to information assets.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular security testing that directly impacts the assessment of data transfer risks by ensuring controls are effective against evolving threats.

requirements.text: Test the adequacy of information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programs
Ensure reviews and tests are commensurate with evolution of threat landscape
Ensure reviews and tests are commensurate with criticality and sensitivity of information assets
Ensure reviews and tests are commensurate with consequences of information security incidents
Ensure reviews and tests are commensurate with risks associated with inability to enforce security policies
Ensure reviews and tests are commensurate with materiality and frequency of change to information assets

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall test the adequacy of its information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programmes. The nature and frequency of the reviews and tests must be commensurate with: (i) the evolution of the threat landscape; (ii) the criticality and sensitivity of the information asset; (iii) the consequences of an information security incident; (iv) the risks associated with exposure to environments where the regulated entity is unable to enforce its information security policies; and (v) the materiality and frequency of change to information assets.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that the results of reviews, audit and testing exercises are
sections.title: Reporting of review and testing results to board
sections.summary: Financial institutions must report the outcomes of reviews, audits, and testing exercises to the board or designated sub-committees and senior management. This ensures oversight of internal risk management activities. The reporting requirement applies to all financial institutions operating under this guideline.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to document and report internal risk assessments to governance bodies, which is critical for assessing the adequacy of internal controls during cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must report the results of reviews, audits, and testing exercises to the board or designated sub-committees of the board and senior management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that the results of reviews, audit and testing exercises are duly reported to the board or designated sub-committees of the board and senior management.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that the assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing
sections.title: Annual review and approval of assurance and testing programmes
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have their assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programmes reviewed and approved by the board or designated sub-committees at least annually or following material changes to information assets or business environment. The internal audit plan must be approved by the audit committee annually or after material changes. This ensures ongoing alignment with risk management needs.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates regular board oversight of risk management frameworks, which is essential for evaluating the robustness of data transfer mechanisms in financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must review and approve assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programmes at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or business environment
Internal audit plan must be approved by the audit committee at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or business environment

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that the assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programme (including the scope) are reviewed and approved by the board or designated sub-committees of the board at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or the business environment. The internal audit plan shall be approved by the audit committee of the board at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or business environment.

sections.heading: The risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall conduct periodic reviews of the
sections.title: Periodic reviews of cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Risk and internal audit functions must periodically review the cyber and technology governance and risk management framework to ensure it aligns with the guideline, relevant standards, and board-approved frameworks. The framework must effectively mitigate cyber risks and ensure compliance with policies and procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular validation of risk management frameworks to ensure they adequately protect data during cross-border transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Risk and internal audit functions must conduct periodic reviews of the cyber and technology governance and risk management framework
Frameworks must be in line with the requirements of this guideline and relevant standards and comply with framework approved by the board and senior management
Frameworks must be effective and adequately mitigate cyber and technology risks faced by the financial institution
All policies, procedures and processes must be duly being complied with

sections.source_quote: The risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall conduct periodic reviews of the cyber and technology governance and risk management framework of the financial institution to, inter alia, ensure that: (i) the frameworks are: a. in line with the requirements of this guideline and relevant standards and in compliance with framework approved by the board and senior management; b. effective and adequately mitigate the cyber and technology risks faced by the financial institution; and (ii) all policies, procedures and processes are duly being complied with.

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance,
sections.title: Parent bank reviews for foreign bank branches
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may rely on reviews conducted by the parent bank's compliance, risk, and internal audit functions. However, they must demonstrate appropriate oversight and control over the scope, frequency, findings, and risk mitigating plans of these reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows foreign banks to leverage parent bank reviews but requires demonstration of oversight, which is relevant for assessing data transfer compliance across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance, risk and internal audit functions of their parent bank
They should be able to demonstrate appropriate oversight and control on the scope, frequency, findings and risk mitigating plans of the reviews conducted by their parent bank

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance, risk and internal audit functions of their parent bank. However, they should be able to demonstrate appropriate oversight and control on the scope, frequency, findings and risk mitigating plans of the reviews conducted by their parent bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management
sections.title: External independent audit of risk management frameworks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited by external independent assessors against the guideline and industry standards. The audit scope and frequency must be risk-based, with critical systems covered annually and all areas within two years for D-SIBs or three years for other institutions. Assessors must report to the board on framework effectiveness and maturity.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires external validation of risk management frameworks, which is critical for ensuring data transfer mechanisms meet international standards and regulatory expectations.

requirements.text: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited by external independent, competent and reputed assessors
The scope and frequency of independent assessment should be risk-based and include IT security audit
Critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets as well as electronic delivery channels shall be covered annually
All areas shall be covered within a two-year cycle for D-SIBs and within a three-year cycle for other financial institutions
Assessors shall report to the board or designated sub-committee on the effectiveness of the cyber and technology risk management framework and its maturity model

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited, against the requirements of the guideline and relevant industry standards, on a periodic basis, by external independent, competent and reputed assessors.

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank
sections.title: Parent bank resources for external audits
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may rely on parent bank resources for conducting external independent audits. However, they must demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows foreign banks to use parent bank resources for audits but requires proof of auditor independence, which is relevant for cross-border data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank for conducting external independent audits
They should be able to demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank for conducting the external independent audits. However, they should be able to demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors.

sections.heading: In cases where the Bank finds it necessary, it may instruct a financial institution to undergo an
sections.title: Bank may require external cyber audit for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Bank of Mauritius may require financial institutions to undergo an external independent audit of their cyber and technology risk management governance, systems, and processes. The financial institution bears the cost of this assessment. This obligation applies to all financial institutions regulated by the Bank.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must undergo external audits for cyber risk management; this affects DTIA by requiring assessment of security controls and processes before data transfers.

requirements.text: The Bank may instruct a financial institution to undergo an external independent audit of its governance, systems and processes related to cyber and technology risk management
The cost of the assessment shall be borne by the financial institution

sections.source_quote: In cases where the Bank finds it necessary, it may instruct a financial institution to undergo an external independent audit of its governance, systems and processes related to cyber and technology risk management. The cost of the assessment shall be borne by the financial institution.

sections.heading: Financial institutions may, in the case of services provided by third-party service providers,
sections.title: Financial institutions may use third-party audit reports for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions can consider third-party service providers' audit reports if they are satisfied with the audit scope, reports, and the independence and competence of the auditors. This applies to services like information asset management where third-party providers conduct audits.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows financial institutions to use third-party audit reports for security assessments, which impacts DTIA by enabling reliance on external audits for data security.

requirements.text: Financial institutions may take into consideration reports of reviews, testing and other audits commissioned by third-party service providers
Where they are satisfied with the plan and scope of the audit/assessment and the reports
And the independence and competence of the auditors/assessors involved

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions may, in the case of services provided by third-party service providers, including the management of information assets, take into consideration reports of reviews, testing and other audits commissioned by the third-party service providers where they are satisfied with, inter alia, the plan and the scope of the audit/assessment and the reports; and the independence and competence of the auditors/assessors involved.

sections.heading: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise
sections.title: Financial institutions must be informed of third-party audit results
sections.summary: Financial institutions must be informed of the results of audits commissioned by third-party service providers and must be satisfied with the remediation plan to address identified security vulnerabilities. This applies to all financial institutions using third-party service providers for information asset management.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to review and approve third-party audit remediation plans, which impacts DTIA by ensuring data security measures are addressed post-audit.

requirements.text: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise commissioned by third-party service providers
And are satisfied with the remediation plan

sections.source_quote: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise commissioned by third-party service providers and are satisfied with the remediation plan.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and
sections.title: Financial institutions must conduct security testing by skilled specialists
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that security testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists, either internal or external. Testing includes vulnerability assessments, scenario-based testing, penetration testing, and red team testing to identify and address security vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific security testing methods for financial institutions, which impacts DTIA by requiring detailed testing protocols for data security.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists (internal to the financial institution or external specialists)
Testing include vulnerability assessment, scenario-based testing, penetration testing and red team testing

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists (internal to the financial institution or external specialists). Testing include vulnerability assessment, scenario-based testing, penetration testing and red team testing.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment
sections.title: Financial institutions must conduct regular vulnerability assessments
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a process for regular vulnerability assessments on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities and address risks in a timely manner. The frequency of assessments should be based on the criticality of the information system and the associated security risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular vulnerability assessments to identify security gaps, which impacts DTIA by ensuring ongoing security evaluations for data transfers.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities and ensure risk arising from these gaps are addressed in a timely manner
The frequency of VA should be commensurate with the criticality of the information system and the security risk to which it is exposed

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment (VA) on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities and ensure risk arising from these gaps are addressed in a timely manner. The frequency of VA should be commensurate with the criticality of the information system and the security risk to which it is exposed.

sections.heading: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial
sections.title: Critical systems must undergo annual vulnerability assessments
sections.summary: Critical systems, services, infrastructures, and assets of financial institutions must be subject to vulnerability assessments by independent specialists at least annually. This ensures that high-risk areas are continuously evaluated for security vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates annual vulnerability assessments for critical systems, which impacts DTIA by requiring specific security evaluations for high-risk data transfers.

requirements.text: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial institution shall be subject to VA by independent specialists on an annual basis

sections.source_quote: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial institution shall be subject to VA by independent specialists on an annual basis.

sections.heading: The scope of the VA should, as a minimum, include vulnerability discovery, identification of
sections.title: Minimum scope for vulnerability assessment
sections.summary: This section mandates that the vulnerability assessment (VA) scope must include identifying weak security configurations, open network ports, and application vulnerabilities. It specifies that web-based systems require checks for common web-based vulnerabilities. The requirements apply to financial institutions conducting VA activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines minimum technical vulnerabilities to assess for data security, directly impacting how financial institutions identify and mitigate risks before data transfers or processing.

requirements.text: The scope of the VA should include vulnerability discovery, identification of weak security configurations, and open network ports
The scope of VA should include checks on common web-based vulnerabilities for web-based systems

sections.source_quote: 75. The scope of the VA should, as a minimum, include vulnerability discovery, identification of weak security configurations, and open network ports, as well as application vulnerabilities. For web-based systems, the scope of VA should include checks on common web-based vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall perform VA before any deployment or redeployment of new or
sections.title: Mandatory vulnerability assessment before deployment
sections.summary: Financial institutions must perform vulnerability assessments before deploying or redeploying new or existing services that support major critical services, applications, and infrastructure components. This includes fixing bugs and weaknesses in line with established change and release management processes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires vulnerability assessments before deploying critical systems, ensuring data security during transfer and processing activities.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall perform VA before deployment or redeployment of new or existing services supporting major critical services, applications and infrastructure components
VA must be conducted to fix bugs and weaknesses consistently with change and release management processes

sections.source_quote: 76. A financial institution shall perform VA before any deployment or redeployment of new or existing services supporting major critical services, applications and infrastructure components for fixing bugs and weaknesses, consistently with change and release management processes in place.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis,
sections.title: Ongoing vulnerability scanning encouraged
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to conduct vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis, rotating among environments to ensure all systems are scanned throughout the year. This practice helps maintain continuous security monitoring without disrupting operations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages regular vulnerability scanning to identify security gaps before data transfers or processing, enhancing the security posture for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis
Vulnerability scanning should rotate among environments to scan all environments throughout the year

sections.source_quote: 77. A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis, rotating among environments in order to scan all environments throughout the year.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual
sections.title: Annual scenario-based testing requirement
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct scenario-based testing exercises at least annually or after major changes in the threat landscape to validate response and recovery plans. This ensures effective handling of cyber threats and technology incidents through communication and coordination with stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates regular scenario testing to validate incident response capabilities, critical for ensuring secure data transfers and processing during cyber incidents.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual basis
Scenario-based testing must be conducted upon major changes in the threat landscape
Testing must validate response and recovery plans against cyber threats and technology incidents

sections.source_quote: 78. A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual basis or upon major changes in the threat landscape to validate its response and recovery, as well as communication plans against cyber threats and technology incidents.

sections.heading: Depending on the exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders,
sections.title: Stakeholder involvement in testing exercises
sections.summary: Financial institutions must involve relevant stakeholders, including senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management teams, service providers, and technical staff, in testing exercises based on the exercise objectives. This ensures comprehensive coverage of cyber threat and technology incident response.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that testing exercises include diverse stakeholders, critical for identifying risks in data transfers and processing across organizational boundaries.

requirements.text: Depending on exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders
Stakeholders include senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management team, service providers, and technical staff

sections.source_quote: 79. Depending on the exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders, including senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management team, service providers, and technical staff responsible for cyber threat and technology incident detection, response and recovery.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its
sections.title: Penetration testing for security evaluation
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct penetration testing to evaluate cyber and technology defences, using a combination of black box and grey box testing. The testing must include both production and test environments to ensure comprehensive security assessment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires penetration testing to identify security weaknesses in data systems, directly impacting the security of data transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its cyber and technology defences
Penetration testing should include both black box and grey box testing
Testing must include both production and test environments

sections.source_quote: 80. A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its cyber and technology defences. A combination of black box and grey box testing should be conducted in this regard. The penetration testing should include both the production and test environment.

sections.heading: The frequency of penetration testing should be determined based on factors such as system
sections.title: Penetration testing frequency based on system criticality and risk exposure
sections.summary: This section mandates that the frequency of penetration testing for financial systems must be determined by system criticality and exposure to cyber risks. Systems directly accessible from the internet or critical infrastructure require immediate testing after deployment or major changes, followed by annual testing. Independent specialists must conduct these tests.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires annual penetration testing for internet-accessible systems and critical infrastructure, with immediate testing post-deployment or major updates. This impacts DTIA by mandating specific testing frequency and independence for high-risk systems.

requirements.text: Penetration testing frequency must be determined based on system criticality and exposure to cyber and technology risks
Systems directly accessible from the internet and critical infrastructure require immediate penetration testing after deployment or major changes
Penetration testing must be conducted annually for systems directly accessible from the internet and critical infrastructure
Penetration testing must be conducted by independent specialists

sections.source_quote: The frequency of penetration testing should be determined based on factors such as system criticality and the system's exposure to cyber and technology risks. For systems that are directly accessible from the internet and for critical infrastructure, assets, systems, penetration testing shall be conducted by independent specialists immediately after deployment or whenever these systems undergo major changes or updates and thereafter on an annual basis.

sections.heading: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to test and validate the effectiveness of their cyber and
sections.title: D-SIBs must conduct red team testing for cyber defense validation
sections.summary: This section requires Designated Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs) to perform red team testing to validate the effectiveness of their cyber and technology defense and response plans against real cyber threats and incidents.
sections.dtia_summary: D-SIBs must validate their cyber defenses through red team testing, which is critical for DTIA as it ensures robust incident response capabilities for high-risk financial entities.

requirements.text: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to test and validate the effectiveness of their cyber and technology defense and response plan
Red team testing must be conducted against prevalent cyber threats and technology incidents

sections.source_quote: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to test and validate the effectiveness of their cyber and technology defence and response plan against prevalent cyber threats and technology incidents.

sections.heading: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing.  Where the Bank finds
sections.title: Encouraged red team testing for other financial institutions with cost responsibility
sections.summary: This section encourages other financial institutions to perform red team testing, but allows the Bank of Mauritius to require such testing if deemed necessary. The financial institution bears the cost of red team testing.
sections.dtia_summary: Other financial institutions are encouraged to conduct red team testing, with the Bank having the authority to mandate it. This affects DTIA by introducing a cost responsibility clause for testing activities.

requirements.text: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing
The Bank may require a financial institution to conduct red team testing if it finds it necessary
The cost of red team testing shall be borne by the financial institution

sections.source_quote: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing. Where the Bank finds it necessary, it may require a financial institution to conduct red team testing. The cost of the red team testing shall be borne by the financial institution.

sections.heading: The objectives, scope and rules of engagement should be defined before the commencement of
sections.title: Red team testing must be controlled and supervised to avoid system disruption
sections.summary: This section requires that the objectives, scope, and rules of engagement for red team testing be defined before commencement, and the exercise must be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision to prevent disruption to production systems.
sections.dtia_summary: Red team testing must be carefully controlled to avoid disrupting financial systems, which is critical for DTIA as it ensures testing does not impact operational stability.

requirements.text: Objectives, scope and rules of engagement should be defined before the commencement of the exercise
The exercise should be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision
Activities carried out by the red team must not disrupt the financial institution's production systems

sections.source_quote: The objectives, scope and rules of engagement should be defined before the commencement of the exercise and the exercise should be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision to ensure the activities carried out by the red team do not disrupt the financial institution's production systems.

sections.heading: The threat scenario should be designed and based on challenging but plausible cyber and
sections.title: Threat scenarios must be challenging but plausible cyber incidents
sections.summary: This section requires that threat scenarios for red team testing be designed to be challenging but plausible cyber and technology incidents, reflecting real-life attacks rather than hypothetical scenarios.
sections.dtia_summary: Threat scenarios must be realistic and challenging, which is essential for DTIA to ensure testing aligns with actual cyber threats faced by financial institutions.

requirements.text: Threat scenarios should be designed and based on challenging but plausible cyber and technology incidents
Threat scenarios should be based on real life attacks

sections.source_quote: The threat scenario should be designed and based on challenging but plausible cyber and technology incidents as well as real life attacks.

sections.heading: The financial institution shall design the exercise scenario by using threat intelligence that is
sections.title: Financial institutions must use threat intelligence for red team scenario design
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial institutions design red team exercise scenarios using threat intelligence relevant to their threat environment to identify potential threat actors and tactics, techniques, and procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must use threat intelligence to design realistic red team scenarios, which is critical for DTIA to ensure testing addresses actual threats.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall design the exercise scenario using threat intelligence relevant to their threat environment
Threat intelligence must identify threat actors most likely to pose a threat to the financial institution
Threat intelligence must identify tactics, techniques and procedures most likely to be used in attacks

sections.source_quote: The financial institution shall design the exercise scenario by using threat intelligence that is relevant to their threat environment to identify threat actors who are most likely to pose a threat to the financial institution and identify the tactics, techniques and procedures most likely to be used in such attacks.

sections.heading: The frequency of the different audits and testing exercises outlined above has been summarised
sections.title: Frequency of cyber security testing exercises
sections.summary: This section specifies minimum testing frequencies for various cyber security activities like vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, and external audits. Financial institutions must conduct these tests at defined intervals to ensure ongoing security of critical systems and infrastructure. The testing schedule varies by activity type and system criticality, with some requiring immediate action after deployment or major changes.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines minimum testing frequencies for cyber security activities that impact data transfer and processing security. Financial institutions must maintain specific testing schedules for critical systems to ensure adequate security during data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Conduct vulnerability scanning immediately after deployment of critical systems
Perform penetration testing for critical systems and internet-accessible assets
Conduct external independent audits of governance frameworks for critical systems every 2 years for D-SIBS and 3 years for other financial institutions
Perform compliance, risk and internal audit reviews every 3 years

sections.source_quote: Minimum testing frequency for vulnerability scanning: Immediately after deployment of critical systems, applications and infrastructure components.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must consider cyber threats for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must consider threats to data confidentiality, privacy, integrity and availability that could damage their reputation. They must include unlikely but plausible cyber events in threat analysis, analyze business and system information to anticipate cyber-attacks, consider threat information from themselves and counterparties, and ensure threat intelligence informs risk mitigation measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to proactively identify and address cyber threats that could impact data transfers and processing security, ensuring threat-informed risk management frameworks.

requirements.text: Consider threats to confidentiality, privacy, integrity and availability of data that could damage reputation
Include threats that could trigger extreme but plausible cyber events, even if unlikely
Analyze cyber threat landscape with internal and external business information to anticipate attacks
Consider threat information from self, counterparties and other financial institutions
Make threat intelligence available to staff responsible for strategic cyber risk management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall consider threats to confidentiality, protection, privacy, integrity and availability of its data and damage to its reputation that could result from such threats.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall establish a communication policy, including a trusted
sections.title: Financial institutions must establish communication policy for information sharing
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a communication policy that includes a trusted channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders, including the Bank. This ensures effective coordination and timely communication during cyber security incidents and risk management activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to have a trusted communication channel for sharing information with stakeholders, which is critical for coordinated data transfer and security incident response.

requirements.text: Establish a communication policy including a trusted channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall establish a communication policy, including a trusted communication channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders, including the Bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall identify the types of information to be shared, as well as the
sections.title: Financial institutions must define information sharing conditions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify the types of information to be shared and the conditions under which information will be shared with relevant stakeholders. This ensures controlled and appropriate data sharing practices.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for information sharing that impact data transfer security and compliance with data sharing requirements.

requirements.text: Identify types of information to be shared with relevant stakeholders
Define conditions under which information will be shared with relevant stakeholders

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall identify the types of information to be shared, as well as the conditions under which the information will be shared, with the relevant stakeholders.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must monitor and improve cyber risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents, analyze this information to determine impact on risk management, monitor technological developments to counter threats, circulate security materials to staff, implement effectiveness indicators for risk management frameworks, and monitor progress toward a more mature risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to continuously monitor and improve their cyber risk management frameworks to ensure secure data transfers and processing, including regular effectiveness assessments.

requirements.text: Gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents within and outside the financial institution
Analyze gathered information to determine impact on cyber risk management framework and review accordingly
Monitor technological developments and cyber risk management processes to counter threats
Circulate relevant cyber security materials to staff when necessary
Implement and regularly monitor indicators to measure effectiveness of cyber risk management framework
Monitor progress in developing and enhancing cyber risk management framework toward a more mature stage

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents both within and outside the financial institution, including both successful intrusions and near misses.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to analyse findings from audits, management information,
sections.title: Financial institutions encouraged to analyze audit findings
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to analyze findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence to identify correlations that could improve their cyber and technology risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Encourages financial institutions to use audit findings to enhance data transfer and processing security by identifying correlations between different risk factors.

requirements.text: Analyze findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to analyse findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence to find any correlation which could be used to improve its cyber and technology risk management framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to have a proactive approach to its cyber and technology
sections.title: Proactive cyber and technology security practices
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to adopt proactive measures for cyber and technology security by predicting future events through analysis of current information and trends. This approach emphasizes anticipating cyber threats and vulnerabilities to enhance organizational resilience. The guidance applies to all financial institutions operating within Mauritius.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages proactive security practices that could influence how financial institutions assess and mitigate cyber risks during data transfers, particularly when handling sensitive financial information across borders.

requirements.text: A financial institution is encouraged to have a proactive approach to its cyber and technology security practices by predicting and anticipating future events, based on analysis of current information and trends.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to have a proactive approach to its cyber and technology security practices by predicting and anticipating future events, based on analysis of current information and trends.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Cybersecurity awareness and training program requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must develop an annual cybersecurity awareness and training program for staff, senior management, and the board. The program must cover incident detection/response, emerging vulnerabilities, and cyber threats. It must be integrated into onboarding for new staff and regularly reviewed to ensure relevance. This requirement applies to all financial institutions in Mauritius.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates regular cybersecurity training that impacts data transfer security by ensuring staff understand threats and vulnerabilities when handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: Develop a robust cyber and technology security awareness and training programme for its staff, senior management and board. The awareness programme should be conducted at least annually.

The awareness programme should at a minimum provide guidance on how to detect and respond to cyber and technology incidents and provide an outline of existing and emerging vulnerabilities and cyber threats and technology incidents.
The awareness and training programme should also be part of the onboarding programme for new staff.
Ensure that there is an appropriate awareness and training programme for staff involved in the cybersecurity and technology risk management.
Regularly review the awareness and training programme to ensure that it incorporates up-to-date information.
Validate the effectiveness of the awareness and training programme on a regular basis.

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) develop a robust cyber and technology security awareness and training programme for its staff, senior management and board. The awareness programme should be conducted at least annually. The awareness programme should at a minimum provide guidance on how to detect and respond to cyber and technology incidents and provide an outline of existing and emerging vulnerabilities and cyber threats and technology incidents. The awareness and training programme should also be part of the onboarding programme for new staff; (ii) ensure that there is an appropriate awareness and training programme for staff involved in the cybersecurity and technology risk management; (iii) regularly review the awareness and training programme to ensure that it incorporates up-to-date information; and (iv) validate the effectiveness of the awareness and training programme on a regular basis.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank the findings of all audits, assessments and testing
sections.title: Submission of audit findings to Bank
sections.summary: Financial institutions must submit audit, assessment, and testing findings along with remediation plans to the Bank within 90 days of completing the exercise. This requirement applies to all financial institutions in Mauritius and ensures timely disclosure of security vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely reporting of security assessments, which is critical for DTIAs as it ensures financial institutions address vulnerabilities before data transfers occur.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank the findings of all audits, assessments and testing exercises together with the remediation plan within 90 days from the date of completion of the respective exercise.

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank the findings of all audits, assessments and testing exercises together with the remediation plan within 90 days from the date of completion of the respective exercise.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall report cyber and technology events and incidents to the Bank in
sections.title: Reporting cyber incidents to Bank
sections.summary: Financial institutions must report all cyber and technology events and incidents to the Bank in the format and manner specified by the Bank. This requirement ensures timely communication of security breaches to regulatory authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates incident reporting, which is crucial for DTIAs as it requires financial institutions to notify regulators of breaches affecting cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall report cyber and technology events and incidents to the Bank in such manner and format prescribed by the Bank.

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall report cyber and technology events and incidents to the Bank in such manner and format prescribed by the Bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall share cyber and technology threat intelligence information with the
sections.title: Sharing threat intelligence with Bank
sections.summary: Financial institutions must share cyber and technology threat intelligence with the Bank in the format and manner specified by the Bank. This requirement promotes collaborative threat intelligence sharing among financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires threat intelligence sharing, which impacts DTIAs by ensuring financial institutions report emerging threats that could affect cross-border data security.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall share cyber and technology threat intelligence information with the Bank in such manner and format prescribed by the Bank.

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall share cyber and technology threat intelligence information with the Bank in such manner and format prescribed by the Bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank a report on the findings of penetration testing
sections.title: Penetration testing reports submission
sections.summary: Financial institutions must submit penetration testing and vulnerability assessment findings for critical systems to the Bank by end-November, along with remediation plans. This requirement applies to all financial institutions in Mauritius.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely submission of penetration testing results, which is critical for DTIAs to assess the security of systems handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank a report on the findings of penetration testing and vulnerability assessments on critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets conducted by independent specialists together with the remediation plan by end-November.

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank a report on the findings of penetration testing and vulnerability assessments on critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets conducted by independent specialists together with the remediation plan by end-November.

sections.title: Financial institutions must submit gap analysis and remediation plan by January 2024
sections.summary: Financial institutions are required to submit a board-approved gap analysis report against this guideline's requirements, along with a remediation plan addressing identified gaps and findings from penetration testing and vulnerability assessments. The report must include timelines and milestones for completion by end-January 2024. This obligation applies to all financial institutions operating under this guideline.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must conduct and report on gap analysis and remediation plans for cyber and technology risk management frameworks to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements before January 2024.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank a board-approved gap analysis report against the requirements of this guideline
Financial institutions shall submit a remediation plan covering the identified gaps and the findings of the penetration testing and vulnerability assessments
The remediation plan must include timelines and milestones for completion by end-January 2024

sections.source_quote: 2023. 101. Financial institutions shall submit to the Bank a board-approved gap analysis report against the requirements of this guideline together with a remediation plan covering the identified gaps and the findings of the penetration testing and vulnerability assessments, with timelines and milestones by end-January 2024.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall ensure that their cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.title: Financial institutions must have externally audited cyber risk management framework by November 2024
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure their cyber and technology risk management framework is audited by an external independent assessor and submit the audit report to the Bank by end-November 2024. The report must assess compliance with this guideline, the effectiveness of the framework, and its maturity against external benchmarks. This requirement applies to all financial institutions operating under this guideline.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must have their cyber and technology risk management frameworks externally audited and report compliance with benchmarks by November 2024, which impacts data security and risk management practices.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall ensure that their cyber and technology risk management framework is duly audited by an external independent assessor
Financial institutions shall submit the audit report to the Bank by end-November 2024
The report should include an assessment on the compliance of the financial institution with the provisions of this guideline
The report should include an assessment on the effectiveness of the cyber and technology risk management framework
The report should include an assessment on the maturity of the framework against a set of relevant external benchmarks

sections.source_quote: 103. Financial institutions shall ensure that their cyber and technology risk management framework is duly audited by an external independent assessor and submit the report to the Bank by end-November 2024. The report should include an assessment on the compliance of the financial institution with the provisions of this guideline, the effectiveness of its cyber and technology risk management framework and its maturity against a set of relevant external benchmarks.
